evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Plaintiff Mirna Acosta, on behalf of the infant plaintiff, commenced this medical malpractice action against defendant NYPH alleging that it failed to timely perform a caesarean section delivery.
evidence_index.summary: Plaintiff alleged NYPH failed to timely perform a caesarean section.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Plaintiff's experts, a retired physician board certified in obstetrics and gynecology, and a physician board certified in pediatrics and child neurology, created an issue of fact by opining that a caesarean section should have been performed within 30 minutes of plaintiff actively bleeding from the previously identified placenta previa.
evidence_index.summary: Plaintiff's experts opined C-section should have been within 30 minutes of bleeding.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's experts

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Judgment, Supreme Court, Bronx County (Alicia Gerez, J.), entered August 22, 2023, dismissing the complaint as against defendant New York Presbyterian Hospital (NYPH), and bringing up for review an order, same court and Justice, entered August 16, 2023, which granted NYPH's motion for summary judgment dismissing the medical malpractice cause of action against it, unanimously reversed, on the law, without costs, the judgment vacated, the motion denied, and the complaint reinstated as against NYPH.
evidence_index.summary: Judgment dismissing complaint against NYPH reversed; complaint reinstated.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: NYPH established, through the medical records and opinion testimony of its experts in maternal-fetal medicine and in neonatal-perinatal medicine, its prima facie case that it did not depart from good and accepted medical practice in its treatment of plaintiff or proximately cause the injuries suffered by the infant plaintiff.
evidence_index.summary: NYPH's experts testified no departure from standard care or causation of injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: NYPH's experts

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: The expert's opinion — that the delay in doing so caused the infant's injuries because the blood loss, whether from fetal or maternal bleeding from placenta previa, reduced the blood supply to the placenta, which resulted in less oxygen to the infant during that delay — falls within this allegation.
evidence_index.summary: Court reasoned delay in C-section caused reduced oxygen to infant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

verdict_detected.key_phrase: Judgment, Supreme Court, Bronx County (Alicia Gerez, J.), entered August 22, 2023, dismissing the complaint

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented with active bleeding from a previously identified placenta previa.

options.clinical_reasoning: Immediate C-section is standard to prevent fetal hypoxia
Delay increases risk of fetal hypoxia due to reduced oxygen supply

expected_action_court.description: Immediate caesarean section should have been performed

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay caused reduced oxygen to infant, leading to injuries

patient_outcome.description: Infant suffered injuries due to reduced oxygen supply

key_findings: Breach of duty in delaying caesarean section
Causation established - delay led to reduced oxygen supply to infant

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to perform timely caesarean section
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Immediate C-section would have prevented hypoxia
